nativeRange.habitat: Clear coastal reefs and protected outer reef habitats with rich invertebrate growth, typically at depths of 20–40 m

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting a varied diet including shaved shrimp, squid, scallop, mysis shrimp, freeze-dried krill soaked in vitamin supplement, and frozen marine algae. Initial acceptance of prepared foods can prove challenging; embedding food in coral rubble has been reported to help encourage consumption. Generally advised to feed at least three times daily.

avoidWith: other filefish of the same genus

imageCandidates.notes: Debra Baker; 2048x1517; striking in-situ specimen with vivid orange body, bluish-purple head and raised file-spine, sharp and diagnostic.
Bernard DUPONT; 2048x1232; clean sharp lateral view showing bluish-black head, orange body and blue-speckled orange caudal fin.
Bernard DUPONT; 2048x1301; whole fish in-situ, orange body and dark grey head, good colour and detail.

summary: The Colored Filefish (Pervagor melanocephalus) is a reef-associated filefish with a striking blackish-brown head and vibrant orange body. Generally considered peaceful, it tends to be shy and is commonly kept singly or in pairs. Initial feeding can require some coaxing.
careNotes: The Colored Filefish is also sold under the names Blackheaded Filefish and Redtail Filefish. It displays a distinctive two-tone coloration: the head and anterior portion of the body are blackish-brown to bluish-black, while the rest of the body is orange-red, with an orange caudal fin and yellow soft dorsal and anal fins. Specimens from Hawaii have been reported to show more vibrant coloration than those collected from the Indian Ocean. This species is generally considered reef-safe, though isolated cases of nipping at small ornamental invertebrates have been reported, and individual variation exists. It tends to be secretive by nature and is often encountered as adult pairs in the wild, typically over deeper reef habitats at 20–40 m. Toward other members of its own genus (Pervagor), it has been known to show aggression; with dissimilar tankmates it tends to be peaceful. On size: aquarium trade sources typically cite a captive adult size of around 10 cm, while scientific records document a maximum total length of 16 cm — this larger figure reflects field-recorded specimens at the upper end of the range, and most captive adults remain smaller. Initial feeding may prove challenging; embedding food items in live rock or coral rubble has been reported to help encourage consumption. A minimum of three feedings daily with varied foods is generally advised.
